attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts (Initial Access)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of existing accounts as a means of gaining initial access to a target environment. Compromised credentials are used to bypass access controls placed on various resources and may even be used to establish initial foothold in a restricted network. Initial access via valid accounts is one of the most common and effective attack vectors because it bypasses many perimeter defenses. Credentials may be obtained through phishing, credential stuffing, password spraying, purchasing from dark web markets, or from previous breaches. This technique is particularly effective against organizations that expose remote services like VPN, RDP, or cloud portals to the internet.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor authentication logs for initial access indicators: first-time logons from new devices or locations, logons from known malicious IPs, and logons during unusual hours. Alert on VPN and remote access service authentication from unexpected geographic locations. Implement impossible travel detection using geo-IP correlation. Monitor Azure AD Identity Protection for risky sign-in events. Track authentication to externally-facing services (VPN, OWA, cloud portals) from IPs associated with TOR, VPN providers, or residential proxies. Correlate with threat intelligence feeds for known compromised credential usage.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The Colonial Pipeline attack (2021) began with a compromised VPN credential. The Uber breach (2022) started with purchased credentials from a contractor's compromised personal device. SolarWinds attackers used valid credentials obtained through supply chain compromise for initial access.

attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts: Default Accounts (Initial Access)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may use default accounts with known credentials to gain initial access to exposed systems and services. Default credentials on internet-facing devices, applications, and cloud services are a primary target for automated scanning and opportunistic attacks. IoT devices, network equipment, web applications, and databases frequently ship with default credentials that administrators fail to change. Shodan and similar services make it trivial to identify internet-facing systems running specific software with known default credentials. Botnets like Mirai automate large-scale exploitation of default credentials.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for authentication attempts using known default usernames (admin, root, administrator, sa, user, default) against externally facing services. Alert on successful authentication with default credentials. Scan all internet-facing services for default credentials during regular vulnerability assessments. Track login attempts from IoT-scanning botnets (known scanner IPs from threat intelligence feeds). Monitor for brute-force attempts against common default credential lists. Alert on first-time authentication from default accounts that should have been disabled or had passwords changed.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The Mirai botnet (2016) compromised over 600,000 IoT devices using 62 default credential pairs, causing the Dyn DNS attack. Default credentials on internet-facing MongoDB, Elasticsearch, and Redis instances have led to numerous data breaches.

attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts: Domain Accounts (Initial Access)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of domain accounts for initial access. Domain account credentials may be obtained through password spraying against externally-facing services that authenticate against Active Directory (VPN, Exchange/OWA, SharePoint, Citrix). Phishing campaigns specifically target domain credentials through fake login pages that mimic corporate SSO portals. Compromised domain credentials from previous breaches may also be used for credential stuffing attacks. Once domain credentials are obtained, they can provide VPN access, email access, and entry into the corporate network.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for domain authentication events on externally-facing services. Alert on successful VPN, OWA, and Citrix Gateway logins from unusual locations or IPs. Track Azure AD sign-in logs for risky sign-ins to externally-facing applications. Monitor for password spraying patterns against AD-authenticated web applications. Correlate external authentication events with internal Active Directory logon events. Alert on domain account usage from geographic locations inconsistent with the employee's normal work location. Implement conditional access policies and monitor for policy bypass attempts.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The Citrix breach (2019) began with password spraying against externally-facing Citrix Gateway. APT33 used password spraying against VPN portals for initial access to US and Saudi Arabian organizations. Many ransomware campaigns begin with compromised VPN credentials.

attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts (Initial Access)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of cloud accounts to gain initial access to cloud environments. Cloud account credentials may be targeted through phishing campaigns using AiTM proxies, password spraying against cloud identity providers, credential stuffing with leaked credentials, or through compromise of on-premises identity infrastructure that synchronizes with cloud directories. Cloud accounts provide direct access to cloud resources regardless of network location, making traditional perimeter defenses ineffective. Initial access through cloud accounts is increasingly common as organizations migrate to cloud services.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor cloud identity provider logs for initial access indicators. Alert on Azure AD sign-in events from unusual locations, new devices, or known-malicious IPs. Track AWS IAM ConsoleLogin events from unexpected source IPs. Monitor for MFA challenge failures followed by successful authentication (potential AiTM). Alert on cloud account access from anonymizing networks. Track OAuth application consent grants from external applications. Monitor for bulk data access following initial cloud account authentication. Implement conditional access policies requiring compliant devices and trusted locations.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Microsoft disclosed Midnight Blizzard (2024) gained initial access via password spraying a legacy test tenant. Storm-0558 used forged tokens for initial cloud access to US government email. The Capital One breach (2019) began with SSRF exploitation against cloud metadata to obtain IAM credentials.

attack_patterns.name: Trusted Relationship
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may breach or otherwise leverage organizations who have access to intended victims. Access through trusted third-party relationships abuses an existing connection that may not be protected or receives less scrutiny than standard mechanisms of gaining access to a network. Third-party relationships include IT service providers, managed security providers, software vendors, and business partners with network access. Organizations often grant elevated access to these trusted parties without applying the same security controls as direct employees. This technique exploits the trust chain between organizations rather than targeting the victim directly.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for authentication from third-party service accounts and partner organization networks. Alert on access patterns from trusted partner accounts that deviate from established baselines (unusual times, unusual resources, unusual data volumes). Track VPN and remote access connections from partner networks for anomalous activity. Monitor for changes to third-party access permissions and network connectivity. Alert on third-party service account usage outside of scheduled maintenance windows. Implement just-in-time access for third-party connections and monitor for persistent access attempts. Track API calls from third-party integrations for anomalous patterns.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The SolarWinds attack (2020) is the definitive example of trusted relationship exploitation through supply chain compromise. The Target breach (2013) originated through a compromised HVAC vendor. Operation Cloud Hopper compromised managed service providers to access their customers.

attack_patterns.name: Phishing: Spearphishing via Service
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may send spearphishing messages via third-party services to gain access to victim systems or credentials. Unlike traditional email-based phishing, this technique uses social media platforms, messaging services, collaboration tools, and other communication services to deliver phishing content. Attackers may send direct messages on LinkedIn, Teams, Slack, or Discord containing malicious links or attachments designed to steal authentication credentials. These platforms are often trusted by users and may bypass email security controls, making them effective for credential harvesting. AiTM phishing via these services is particularly effective for bypassing MFA.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor messaging platform logs for suspicious message patterns and URL sharing. Alert on OAuth application authorization requests initiated from messaging platform links. Track click-through rates on external URLs shared via collaboration platforms. Monitor for user credential submissions to known phishing infrastructure. Alert on AiTM phishing indicators: authentication flows passing through unexpected intermediate domains. Implement link scanning and URL reputation checking for messages in collaboration platforms. Monitor for suspicious OAuth consent grants following message interactions.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Lapsus$ group used social engineering via messaging platforms to recruit insiders and obtain credentials. The Uber breach (2022) began with social engineering on Slack. LinkedIn phishing is widely used by North Korean threat actors (Lazarus Group) for initial access.

attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts (Defense Evasion)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may use valid accounts to evade detection and blend in with normal user activity. By using compromised legitimate accounts rather than creating new accounts or deploying malware, adversaries can operate within the expected bounds of normal user behavior. This makes their activities much harder to distinguish from legitimate operations. Defense evasion through valid accounts includes using different compromised accounts for different phases of an attack, timing activities to coincide with normal business operations, and avoiding actions that would trigger security alerts associated with unauthorized access patterns.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Implement User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA) to establish behavioral baselines and detect anomalous activity from valid accounts. Monitor for account usage patterns that deviate from established baselines: unusual access times, unusual resources accessed, unusual data volumes, and unusual application usage. Alert on accounts performing actions inconsistent with their role or job function. Track accounts accessing resources during non-business hours. Monitor for accounts that suddenly begin accessing sensitive data they have never accessed before. Implement peer group analysis to detect accounts behaving differently from their peers.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT29 blended in with normal user activity using compromised accounts during the SolarWinds campaign, evading detection for months. Most advanced persistent threats use valid accounts for defense evasion as a fundamental tradecraft element.

attack_patterns.name: Create Account
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may create an account to maintain access to victim systems. With a sufficient level of access, creating new accounts is a straightforward persistence mechanism that allows adversaries to bypass dependence on compromised credentials that may be discovered and reset. Created accounts can be used to maintain access to victim environments, especially when combined with privilege escalation techniques to grant the new account administrative permissions. Adversaries may create accounts in various environments including local operating systems, Active Directory domains, cloud tenants, and SaaS applications.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for account creation events across all identity platforms. Alert on Windows Event IDs 4720 (user account created) and 4722 (user account enabled). Track Azure AD audit logs for 'Add user' events. Monitor AWS IAM for CreateUser and CreateServiceAccount API calls. Alert on account creation outside of approved HR onboarding workflows. Implement change management for account creation with approval workflows. Monitor for accounts created with administrative privileges. Track account creation events during non-business hours or from unusual source systems. Alert on accounts created without corresponding HR ticket or onboarding request.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT29 created new accounts in Azure AD for persistent access during the SolarWinds campaign. Ransomware operators routinely create local administrator accounts for persistence. The Hafnium group created accounts on compromised Exchange servers.

attack_patterns.name: Create Account: Local Account
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may create a local account to maintain access to victim systems. Local accounts provide persistent access to individual systems and can be configured with administrative privileges. Adversaries typically create local accounts during post-exploitation to ensure they maintain access even if their original compromise vector is discovered and remediated. These accounts may be named to blend in with legitimate service accounts or administrative accounts. On Windows, local accounts can be created using net user, PowerShell New-LocalUser, or directly through the SAM database.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor Windows Event ID 4720 (user account created) for local account creation. Alert on net user /add and New-LocalUser PowerShell commands via command-line logging (Event ID 4688 with command-line auditing). Track additions to local administrator group (Event ID 4732 for Administrators group). Monitor Linux /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow for new entries using file integrity monitoring. Alert on useradd and adduser command execution. Implement alerts for local account creation on servers that should only use domain accounts. Track account creation events outside of approved provisioning workflows.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Most ransomware families create local administrator accounts for persistence. The DarkSide ransomware (Colonial Pipeline) created local accounts. APT groups routinely create local accounts named to resemble legitimate service accounts.

attack_patterns.name: Create Account: Domain Account
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may create a domain account to maintain access to victim networks. Domain accounts are managed by Active Directory and can be used to log into any domain-joined system. Creating domain accounts requires sufficient privileges (typically Domain Admin or Account Operator). Adversaries may create domain accounts that mimic legitimate service or administrative accounts to blend in with normal directory activity. Domain accounts created for persistence may be placed in privileged groups to ensure continued elevated access. These accounts survive individual system reimaging and provide network-wide persistence.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor Windows Event ID 4720 (user account created) on domain controllers. Alert on account creation by accounts other than approved provisioning service accounts. Track Event IDs 4728/4732/4756 for additions to privileged security groups (Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, Administrators). Monitor for accounts created with servicePrincipalName attributes (potential Kerberoasting targets). Alert on domain account creation outside of HR onboarding workflows. Implement mandatory attributes for new accounts (department, manager) and alert on accounts created without these attributes. Monitor for accounts with names similar to existing service accounts.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT29 created domain accounts during the SolarWinds compromise for persistent network access. Multiple ransomware operators create domain accounts with administrative privileges. The Hafnium group created domain accounts on compromised Exchange server organizations.

attack_patterns.name: Create Account: Cloud Account
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may create a cloud account to maintain access to victim cloud environments. Cloud accounts can include user accounts in Azure AD/Entra ID, AWS IAM users, GCP accounts, and service principals/applications. Cloud account creation for persistence is particularly effective because cloud accounts often provide access to resources regardless of network location, surviving traditional remediation steps like network isolation. Adversaries may create service principals with API permissions, guest accounts with elevated roles, or federated identity configurations that enable persistent access through external identity providers.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor Azure AD audit logs for 'Add user', 'Invite external user', and 'Add service principal' events. Alert on cloud account creation by non-provisioning accounts. Track AWS IAM CreateUser, CreateRole, and CreateServiceAccount events in CloudTrail. Monitor GCP audit logs for IAM user and service account creation. Alert on guest user invitations from unexpected sources. Track service principal creation and API permission grants. Monitor for cloud accounts created with permanent role assignments bypassing PIM. Alert on federated identity credential configurations on applications.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT29 created Azure AD service principals during the SolarWinds campaign. Storm-0558 created cloud accounts for persistent access to Microsoft 365 environments. Cloud account creation for persistence is increasingly common in cloud-focused attacks.

attack_patterns.name: Account Manipulation (Persistence)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may manipulate existing accounts to maintain persistent access to victim systems and cloud environments. This includes adding new credentials to existing accounts (passwords, certificates, API keys), modifying account permissions and role assignments, changing email forwarding rules, and configuring SSH authorized keys. Unlike creating new accounts, manipulating existing accounts is stealthier because the account already exists in the directory and has an established usage history. Changes to existing accounts may be less likely to trigger security alerts than creation of entirely new accounts.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for account modification events that could indicate persistence establishment. Alert on Windows Event ID 4738 (user account changed) and Event ID 4670 (permissions on an object changed). Track Azure AD audit logs for credential changes to service principals and applications. Monitor for SSH authorized_keys modifications. Alert on email forwarding rule changes, especially to external addresses. Track MFA method registrations and changes for all accounts. Monitor for role assignment changes that grant additional privileges. Implement change management workflows and alert on modifications outside approved processes.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT29 added credentials to Azure AD service principals for persistent access during the SolarWinds campaign. Lapsus$ modified account permissions for persistence. BEC actors routinely add email forwarding rules for persistent email access.

attack_patterns.name: Modify Authentication Process (Persistence)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may modify authentication mechanisms to maintain persistent access that survives credential rotation and standard remediation procedures. By embedding backdoors in the authentication infrastructure itself, adversaries ensure that their access persists regardless of password resets, MFA changes, or account modifications. This includes installing modified PAM modules, registering malicious password filter DLLs, patching domain controller authentication, adding rogue AD FS components, and modifying cloud federation configurations. Authentication process modifications represent some of the most persistent and difficult-to-detect backdoors in enterprise environments.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor authentication infrastructure components for unauthorized modifications. Alert on changes to PAM modules and configuration files (/etc/pam.d/*). Track new DLLs registered as password filters or notification packages on domain controllers. Monitor AD FS configuration changes and signing certificate modifications. Alert on Azure AD federation configuration changes. Implement file integrity monitoring on all authentication infrastructure components. Track authentication service restart events that may indicate module replacement. Monitor for authentication patterns indicating backdoor usage (e.g., authentication without corresponding credential validation events).
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT29 installed FoggyWeb and MagicWeb backdoors in AD FS during the SolarWinds campaign, providing persistent authentication bypass. The Ebury operation modified SSH and PAM components for persistent credential harvesting across thousands of servers.

attack_patterns.name: Use Alternate Authentication Material (Persistence)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may maintain persistence by using alternate authentication material such as forged Kerberos tickets, stolen OAuth refresh tokens, forged SAML tokens, or cloned session cookies. Unlike one-time credential theft, this technique involves maintaining a reservoir of authentication material that provides ongoing access. Long-lived refresh tokens, Golden Tickets with extended lifetimes, and forged SAML tokens with custom validity periods all provide persistent access that survives password resets. In cloud environments, OAuth refresh tokens may provide access for weeks or months, while Golden Tickets can persist indefinitely if the KRBTGT hash is not rotated.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for authentication material with anomalous properties: Kerberos tickets with unusually long lifetimes, OAuth tokens used after associated sessions have been terminated, and SAML assertions from unexpected issuers. Track refresh token usage patterns for accounts that have had their passwords reset. Alert on Golden Ticket indicators: TGTs with lifetimes exceeding domain policy. Monitor for token usage from revoked or disabled accounts. Track authentication events that bypass normal credential validation flows. Implement token revocation monitoring and alert on continued token usage after revocation.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT29 used Golden Tickets and forged SAML tokens for persistent access during the SolarWinds campaign. Storm-0558 maintained persistent access through forged authentication tokens. Long-lived OAuth refresh tokens have been exploited for persistent access in multiple cloud breaches.

attack_patterns.name: Modify Authentication Process (Defense Evasion)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may modify authentication mechanisms to evade detection by ensuring their access does not generate standard authentication alerts. By controlling the authentication process itself, adversaries can suppress logging, modify audit events, or ensure their authentication bypass does not trigger security monitoring. This defense evasion angle of authentication modification focuses on stealth rather than persistence: the adversary modifies authentication infrastructure to make their access invisible to security monitoring rather than merely maintaining access. This includes modifying authentication event logging, disabling audit trail generation, and creating authentication paths that bypass monitored channels.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for gaps in authentication logging that may indicate log suppression. Alert on authentication infrastructure configuration changes that affect audit logging. Track changes to audit policies on domain controllers and identity providers. Monitor for authentication events that appear in some logs but not others (indicating selective log tampering). Implement log integrity verification (signing, chaining) for authentication logs. Alert on changes to AD FS logging configuration. Monitor for modifications to cloud audit log settings. Implement out-of-band authentication monitoring that cannot be suppressed by on-system modifications.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT29 modified AD FS to suppress logging during the SolarWinds campaign. Advanced threat actors routinely modify authentication logging to evade detection. FoggyWeb malware exfiltrated AD FS configuration while evading standard monitoring.

attack_patterns.name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools (Defense Evasion)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may modify and/or disable security tools to avoid possible detection of their malware and activities. This includes disabling security monitoring for identity and access management systems such as Azure AD Identity Protection, Microsoft Defender for Identity, CrowdStrike Falcon Identity Protection, and SIEM rules that detect authentication anomalies. By disabling these identity-focused security tools, adversaries can conduct credential access, privilege escalation, and lateral movement activities without triggering alerts. This technique is often one of the first actions taken after gaining privileged access to ensure subsequent activities go undetected.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for changes to security tool configurations, especially disabling or modifying identity protection tools. Alert on Azure AD audit logs for changes to Identity Protection policies, Conditional Access policies, and diagnostic settings. Track Microsoft Defender for Identity sensor status and configuration changes. Monitor for disabling of authentication logging on domain controllers. Alert on SIEM rule modifications that affect authentication monitoring. Track changes to audit policies using Windows Event ID 4719 (system audit policy changed). Implement out-of-band monitoring for security tool health status.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Ransomware operators routinely disable security tools before deploying ransomware. APT29 disabled security monitoring during the SolarWinds campaign. The Conti ransomware group specifically targeted Defender for Identity and SIEM integrations.

attack_patterns.name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Cloud Logs (Defense Evasion)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may disable or modify cloud logging capabilities and integrations to limit what data is collected on their activities and avoid detection. Cloud environments typically provide built-in logging for identity and access events (Azure AD audit logs, AWS CloudTrail, GCP audit logs), but these can be disabled or modified by adversaries with sufficient privileges. Disabling cloud audit logging for identity operations removes visibility into credential access, privilege escalation, and lateral movement activities. Adversaries may also modify log retention settings, delete existing logs, or redirect log streams to reduce the effectiveness of security monitoring.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for changes to cloud logging configurations at the highest priority. Alert on Azure diagnostic settings modifications, especially disabling Azure AD audit logs or sign-in logs. Track AWS CloudTrail for StopLogging, DeleteTrail, and UpdateTrail events. Monitor GCP for setIamPolicy calls that remove audit log viewer permissions. Alert on log retention period reductions. Track log forwarding configuration changes (SIEM integrations, Log Analytics workspace). Monitor for large-scale log deletion events. Implement immutable log forwarding to a security-owned environment that cannot be modified by cloud administrators.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Storm-0558 exploited gaps in Microsoft cloud logging that limited customer visibility into the attack. APT29 modified logging configurations during the SolarWinds compromise. Multiple cloud breaches have involved disabling CloudTrail to hide IAM-related activities.

attack_patterns.name: Modify Authentication Process: Domain Controller Authentication (Persistence)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may patch the authentication process on a domain controller to create persistent backdoor access. By modifying the Local Security Authority Subsystem Service (LSASS) on domain controllers, adversaries can install persistent authentication backdoors that accept a universal password for any domain account. The Skeleton Key attack, first documented in 2015, patches the domain controller's LSASS process in memory to accept a master password alongside legitimate user passwords, providing persistent access to every domain account without disrupting normal authentication for legitimate users. This persistence mechanism survives password resets but not domain controller reboots unless additional persistence mechanisms are employed.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor domain controller LSASS process integrity using Protected Process Light (PPL) and Credential Guard. Alert on Sysmon Event ID 7 (Image loaded) for unexpected DLLs loaded by lsass.exe on domain controllers. Track process access events (Sysmon Event ID 10) targeting LSASS. Monitor for Skeleton Key indicators: Event ID 7045 (new service) and Event ID 4673 (privileged service) on domain controllers. Implement domain controller behavior monitoring with Microsoft Defender for Identity. Perform regular LSASS memory integrity verification.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The Skeleton Key malware was discovered by Dell SecureWorks in 2015. Multiple APT groups have patched domain controller authentication for persistent access. The technique requires domain admin privileges and is used as a persistence mechanism after initial domain compromise.

attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts (Persistence)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may maintain access through compromised valid accounts as a persistence mechanism. By retaining access to stolen credentials or authentication material, adversaries can re-establish access even after other persistence mechanisms are discovered and removed. This includes maintaining access to stolen passwords, refresh tokens, session cookies, SSH keys, API keys, and service account credentials. Valid account persistence is difficult to detect because the adversary uses legitimate authentication pathways that generate expected audit events. Persistence through valid accounts may also involve preventing the target from changing compromised credentials.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for account usage patterns that suggest persistent unauthorized access. Alert on accounts being used from previously unknown devices or locations after a security incident. Track password reset events and correlate with subsequent authentication patterns. Monitor for accounts that authenticate immediately after credential reset events from unexpected sources. Implement credential rotation monitoring and alert on accounts whose credentials have not been rotated after a security incident. Track OAuth refresh token usage for accounts that should have had tokens revoked. Monitor for MFA re-registration events following security incidents.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT29 maintained access through compromised credentials during the SolarWinds campaign. Most advanced persistent threats maintain multiple sets of compromised credentials for persistence. The Midnight Blizzard Microsoft breach (2024) involved persistent access through compromised test tenant credentials.

attack_patterns.name: Account Manipulation: Additional Cloud Credentials (Persistence)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may add adversary-controlled credentials to cloud accounts to maintain persistent access even after original compromised credentials are rotated. By adding new client secrets, certificates, or federated identity credentials to Azure AD service principals, AWS IAM roles, or GCP service accounts, adversaries create alternative authentication paths that survive remediation of the original compromise. The adversary can then authenticate using the new credentials at any time. This is one of the most common and effective cloud persistence techniques because service principals and applications often have many credentials, making the addition of one more difficult to notice.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor Azure AD audit logs for 'Add service principal credentials' and 'Update application - Certificates and secrets management' events, especially for applications with high-privilege API permissions. Alert on new credential additions to service principals that don't match approved deployment workflows. Track credential additions that occur outside of CI/CD pipeline schedules. Monitor for credentials with unusually long expiration periods. Alert on credential additions to applications by users who are not listed as application owners. Track AWS IAM CreateAccessKey events for service accounts. Implement regular credential inventory reviews.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT29 added credentials to Azure AD applications during the SolarWinds compromise for persistent access to victim cloud environments. This technique is consistently identified in cloud security assessments as a common persistence method.

attack_patterns.name: Account Manipulation: Additional Cloud Roles (Persistence)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may assign additional roles or permissions to compromised cloud accounts to establish persistent elevated access. By granting permanent Global Administrator, Owner, or other privileged roles to attacker-controlled accounts, adversaries ensure they maintain the highest level of access to cloud resources. This persistence is difficult to remediate because the elevated accounts can undo any remediation steps, re-grant their own roles, and create additional backdoor accounts. In Azure AD, adversaries may exploit Privileged Identity Management (PIM) by making their role assignments permanent (non-eligible) or by modifying PIM policy to extend activation durations.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for privileged role assignments in cloud environments. Alert on Azure AD 'Add member to role' events for Global Administrator, Privileged Role Administrator, and Application Administrator roles. Track PIM configuration changes that modify role assignment policies. Monitor for permanent role assignments that bypass PIM eligible assignment workflows. Alert on role assignments to accounts that were recently created or recently compromised. Track AWS IAM policy attachments for AdministratorAccess and other privileged policies. Implement alerts for any modifications to the PIM configuration itself.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT29 assigned elevated cloud roles to compromised accounts during the SolarWinds campaign. The technique is a standard component of cloud-focused attack chains documented by Microsoft Threat Intelligence.

attack_patterns.name: Account Manipulation: Device Registration (Persistence)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may register rogue devices in the victim's identity provider to maintain persistent access. By registering an adversary-controlled device as a compliant or hybrid-joined device in Azure AD, the adversary can satisfy device-based conditional access policies and maintain access even after the user's credentials are reset. Registered devices may also receive MDM-enrolled certificates that provide certificate-based authentication, creating an additional authentication path. Device registration persistence is particularly effective because device trust is often evaluated independently of user credential changes.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor Azure AD audit logs for device registration events, especially for privileged accounts. Alert on device registrations from unexpected platforms, operating systems, or geographic locations. Track Intune enrollment events for devices that don't match organizational hardware standards. Monitor for device registrations that occur outside of standard device provisioning workflows. Alert on multiple device registrations for a single user in a short time period. Track device compliance state changes and alert on newly compliant devices from unusual sources. Monitor for certificate enrollment events following device registration.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Azure AD device registration abuse has been demonstrated by security researchers and implemented in the AADInternals toolkit. The technique has been used in real-world attacks to bypass conditional access policies requiring compliant devices.

attack_patterns.name: Server Software Component: Terminal Services DLL (Persistence)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may abuse components of Terminal Services to maintain persistent access to systems. Terminal Services, or Remote Desktop Services (RDS), allows users to connect to remote systems via RDP. Adversaries may modify Terminal Services DLLs or configuration to create backdoor access that is invoked whenever a user connects via RDP. This includes modifying the termsrv.dll to enable concurrent RDP sessions, installing RDP wrapper libraries that bypass licensing restrictions, or modifying RDP authentication DLLs to capture credentials. These modifications persist across reboots and provide passive credential collection and backdoor access.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor file integrity of Terminal Services DLLs, especially termsrv.dll and related authentication DLLs in the %SystemRoot%\System32 directory. Alert on modifications to RDP-related registry keys (HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Terminal Server). Track Sysmon Event ID 7 for unusual DLLs loaded by svchost.exe hosting the TermService. Monitor for RDP Wrapper Library installation indicators. Alert on changes to RDP authentication settings and Network Level Authentication (NLA) configuration. Track RDP session events (Event IDs 4624 Type 10, 1149) for sessions authenticated with modified authentication components.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: RDP backdoor DLLs have been used by multiple APT groups for persistent RDP access. The RDP Wrapper Library is commonly deployed by threat actors to enable concurrent sessions and bypass authentication. Sticky Keys and Utility Manager RDP accessibility backdoors remain common persistence techniques.

attack_patterns.name: Modify Authentication Process: Hybrid Identity (Persistence)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may modify hybrid identity configurations to establish persistent access across both on-premises and cloud environments. Azure AD Connect and similar hybrid identity solutions synchronize identities between on-premises Active Directory and cloud directories. By compromising the synchronization service, modifying sync rules, or manipulating the pass-through authentication (PTA) agent, adversaries can create persistent backdoor access that spans both environments. The Azure AD Connect service account has significant privileges in both AD and Azure AD, and its compromise provides a bridge for persistence that survives remediation efforts focused on either environment alone.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor Azure AD Connect server integrity and configuration changes. Alert on modifications to sync rules and connector configurations in the ADSync database. Track Azure AD Connect service account activity for anomalous operations. Monitor for changes to PTA agent configurations and new PTA agent registrations. Alert on modifications to password hash synchronization settings. Track Azure AD audit logs for changes originating from the Azure AD Connect sync account. Monitor for unauthorized access to the Azure AD Connect configuration database. Implement privileged access workstation requirements for Azure AD Connect management.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Multiple APT groups have targeted Azure AD Connect for hybrid identity compromise. The AADInternals toolkit demonstrates various hybrid identity persistence techniques. MERCURY (MuddyWater) has targeted Azure AD Connect in Middle Eastern campaigns.

attack_patterns.name: Access Token Manipulation (Defense Evasion)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may modify access tokens to operate under a different user or system security context for defense evasion purposes. By assuming the identity of a legitimate user through token manipulation, adversaries can make their malicious activities appear as normal operations by a trusted account. This defense evasion technique allows adversaries to bypass access controls and avoid triggering security alerts that would be generated by their own compromised account. Token manipulation for defense evasion focuses on blending in with legitimate user activity rather than gaining additional privileges, though the two goals are often complementary.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for processes whose security context changes during execution, which may indicate token theft or impersonation. Alert on processes that perform privileged operations under a user token that was not obtained through interactive or network logon. Track token manipulation API calls (DuplicateTokenEx, ImpersonateLoggedOnUser, SetThreadToken) from non-standard processes. Monitor for behavioral anomalies where a process's actions do not match the expected behavior of the user whose token it is using. Implement process-level auditing that tracks security context changes throughout process lifetime.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT groups routinely use token manipulation for defense evasion during lateral movement. Cobalt Strike's token commands are designed specifically for defense evasion by impersonating legitimate user tokens. Metasploit's incognito module provides token-based defense evasion capabilities.

attack_patterns.name: Domain or Tenant Policy Modification: Trust Modification (Defense Evasion)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may modify trust relationships or federation configurations to evade detection while maintaining access. By adding trusted identity providers, modifying SAML federation settings, or creating cross-domain trust relationships, adversaries can authenticate through channels that may not be monitored by the victim's security infrastructure. Authentication through federated trusts may bypass conditional access policies, MFA requirements, and identity protection monitoring that apply to direct authentication. This defense evasion technique creates authentication paths that exist outside the victim's normal identity monitoring scope.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for all changes to federation trust configurations and domain trust relationships. Alert on new trusted identity providers added to Azure AD custom domains. Track AD FS relying party trust modifications and new claims rules. Monitor for changes to SAML metadata and token signing certificates. Alert on new forest and domain trusts created in Active Directory (Windows Event ID 4706). Track modifications to conditional access policies that exempt federated authentication. Monitor for authentication events from newly trusted identity providers. Implement integrity monitoring on federation configuration stores.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT29 modified Azure AD federation trust configurations during the SolarWinds campaign to enable Golden SAML attacks that bypassed normal authentication monitoring. Trust modification for defense evasion is a hallmark of advanced nation-state operations.

attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts: Local Accounts (Persistence)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may maintain persistence by retaining access to compromised local account credentials or creating hidden local accounts. Local accounts on critical systems such as servers, network devices, and databases provide persistent access that is independent of domain credential changes. Adversaries may modify local account properties to prevent password expiration, hide accounts from login screens, or disable account lockout to maintain long-term access. On Windows, local accounts can be hidden from the logon screen by modifying the UserList registry key. On Linux, accounts can be hidden from user enumeration by setting UID below the login threshold.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for local account property modifications that suggest persistence establishment. Alert on Windows Event ID 4738 (user account changed) for local accounts with password-never-expires flag set. Track registry modifications to HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\SpecialAccounts\UserList for hidden accounts. Monitor Linux /etc/passwd for accounts with UIDs below the login threshold or unusual shell assignments. Alert on local account logon events from unexpected source IPs. Track local account password changes that occur outside of approved maintenance windows.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Ransomware operators commonly create hidden local admin accounts for persistent access. APT groups configure local accounts with non-expiring passwords on compromised servers. Multiple intrusions have involved hidden local accounts persisting for months before detection.

attack_patterns.name: Account Manipulation: SSH Authorized Keys (Persistence)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may modify the SSH authorized_keys file to maintain persistent access to victim systems via SSH key authentication. Adding an adversary-controlled public key to a user's or service account's authorized_keys file provides password-less SSH access that survives password changes, does not generate password-based authentication logs, and persists across system reboots. This technique is particularly prevalent in cloud environments where SSH is the primary access method for Linux instances. Adversaries may add keys to multiple accounts across multiple systems to create redundant access paths.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Implement file integrity monitoring specifically targeting ~/.ssh/authorized_keys and /etc/ssh/authorized_keys* files across all Linux systems. Alert on any additions to authorized_keys files, particularly for root and service accounts. Maintain an inventory of authorized SSH public keys and alert on keys not in the inventory. Monitor for SSH key authentication events where the authenticating key is not in the managed key inventory. Track mass modifications to authorized_keys across multiple systems. Alert on authorized_keys changes that occur outside of approved provisioning workflows. Monitor for SSH sessions from IP addresses associated with newly added keys.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The Ebury botnet added SSH keys to thousands of compromised Linux servers for persistent access spanning years. Cloud infrastructure breaches frequently involve SSH authorized_keys manipulation for persistence. APT campaigns targeting Linux infrastructure routinely install SSH keys for long-term access.

attack_patterns.name: Create Account: Local Account (Persistence via Hidden Account)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may create hidden local accounts specifically designed to evade detection while providing persistent system access. Beyond simply creating local accounts, adversaries employ various techniques to hide these accounts: setting the UserList registry key to 0 on Windows, using UIDs below the login UID threshold on Linux, naming accounts to mimic system services, or using Unicode characters that resemble legitimate account names. These hidden accounts often have administrative privileges and non-expiring passwords, providing reliable backdoor access that is difficult to discover during routine account audits.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for local account creation events and immediately verify against approved provisioning workflows. Track Windows Event ID 4720 with correlation to UserList registry modifications. Monitor for accounts with names that closely resemble system service accounts. Scan for accounts with non-standard UIDs on Linux systems. Alert on net user /add commands with the /active:no flag or subsequent account hiding operations. Implement periodic local account audits comparing against authorized account lists. Monitor for accounts that appear in SAM but not in standard user enumeration commands.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Multiple ransomware families including LockBit and BlackCat create hidden local accounts for persistence. APT41 has created hidden accounts named to resemble system services. The technique is frequently discovered during incident response engagements.

attack_patterns.name: Create Account: Domain Account (Persistence via Stealthy Placement)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may create domain accounts in obscure OUs or with names that blend in with existing service accounts to maintain persistent network access. Domain accounts for persistence may be placed in seldom-audited organizational units, given names matching the organization's service account naming convention, and gradually granted permissions over time to avoid triggering alerts on sudden privilege grants. Some adversaries create multiple domain accounts as redundant access paths, ensuring that the compromise of one persistence mechanism does not eliminate all access.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor Windows Event ID 4720 on domain controllers with correlation to HR onboarding systems. Alert on account creation in OUs that typically contain only service accounts. Track accounts created with properties matching service account patterns but without corresponding service deployment records. Monitor for domain accounts with no associated manager or department attributes. Alert on accounts created by accounts that are not in the authorized provisioning group. Implement daily reconciliation of Active Directory accounts against HR systems.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT29 created accounts mimicking legitimate service accounts in Active Directory. Multiple advanced threat actors use domain account creation in obscure OUs for long-term persistence. The technique was observed in the SolarWinds campaign.

attack_patterns.name: Modify Authentication Process: Password Filter DLL (Persistence)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may register a malicious password filter DLL as a persistence mechanism that passively captures every password change in the domain. Password filter DLLs are loaded by LSASS on domain controllers and receive plaintext passwords during password change operations. A malicious password filter provides persistent credential access because it automatically captures new passwords as users rotate their credentials, effectively defeating password rotation as a remediation measure. The DLL persists across domain controller reboots through registry configuration and continues operating silently as long as the domain controller is running.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Regularly audit the registry key HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\Notification Packages for unexpected entries. Verify the digital signature and file hash of all registered password filter DLLs. Monitor for new DLL files in the System32 directory on domain controllers. Alert on Sysmon Event ID 7 for new DLLs loaded by lsass.exe. Implement application whitelisting on domain controllers. Track registry modifications to LSA configuration keys using Windows Event ID 4657. Monitor for outbound network connections from lsass.exe on domain controllers.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Password filter DLLs for persistent credential harvesting have been discovered in nation-state operations targeting government networks. The technique is documented in multiple threat intelligence reports as a high-impact persistence mechanism on domain controllers.

attack_patterns.name: Modify Authentication Process: Pluggable Authentication Modules (Persistence)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may install modified Pluggable Authentication Modules (PAM) to maintain persistent access to Linux and Unix systems. Modified PAM modules can be configured to log plaintext credentials to a file, accept a hardcoded backdoor password alongside legitimate passwords, or bypass authentication entirely for specific usernames. PAM module modifications persist across system reboots and provide both credential harvesting and backdoor access capabilities. Because PAM modules are invoked for every authentication event (SSH, sudo, su, login), a compromised PAM module provides comprehensive visibility into all credential usage on the system.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Implement file integrity monitoring on all PAM module shared objects in /lib/security/ and /lib64/security/. Monitor PAM configuration files (/etc/pam.d/*) for unauthorized modifications. Compare PAM module checksums against known-good values from distribution packages using rpm -V or dpkg --verify. Monitor for compiler execution (gcc, make) on production systems that may indicate PAM module compilation. Track PAM authentication events for anomalous patterns such as successful authentication without password prompts. Monitor for outbound data exfiltration from systems with modified PAM modules.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The Ebury/Windigo operation modified PAM modules on thousands of Linux servers for persistent credential harvesting spanning multiple years. Multiple APT groups targeting Linux infrastructure have deployed modified PAM modules for persistent backdoor access.

attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts (Persistence)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may maintain persistent access to cloud environments through compromised or adversary-created cloud accounts. Cloud account persistence is particularly effective because cloud accounts provide access regardless of network location, are often not subject to the same monitoring as on-premises accounts, and may have long-lived credentials or refresh tokens. Adversaries may maintain access through multiple cloud identity paths: user accounts with saved browser sessions, service principals with multiple credential sets, managed identity abuse, and guest accounts with elevated permissions. Cloud account persistence can survive on-premises remediation since cloud and on-premises identity systems may not be fully synchronized.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor cloud identity provider logs for persistent access indicators. Track Azure AD sign-in logs for accounts that authenticate from unusual patterns after a security incident. Alert on continued access from disabled or password-reset accounts via cached tokens. Monitor for service principal authentication using credentials that should have been rotated. Track OAuth refresh token usage for revoked applications. Alert on guest account access to resources after the inviting user relationship has been terminated. Implement continuous access evaluation (CAE) and monitor for token revocation failures.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Storm-0558 maintained persistent cloud access through forged tokens. APT29 maintained cloud persistence through multiple credential sets on service principals. The Midnight Blizzard Microsoft breach (2024) demonstrated long-term cloud persistence through a compromised test tenant.

attack_patterns.name: Trusted Relationship (Persistence via Third-Party Access)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may abuse trusted third-party relationships to maintain persistent access to victim environments. Once an adversary compromises a third-party service provider, managed service provider, or business partner with trusted access, they can use this relationship as a persistent entry point to the victim's environment. The trusted relationship often involves dedicated VPN tunnels, federated identity configurations, API integrations, or service accounts with elevated privileges. This persistence is particularly resilient because remediation focused on the victim environment alone does not address the compromised third party, and the trusted access may be re-established through normal business processes.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor all third-party access channels for anomalous activity patterns. Alert on third-party service account usage outside of scheduled maintenance windows. Track VPN connections from partner networks for unusual timing, data volumes, or destination access patterns. Monitor API calls from third-party integrations for resource access outside their normal scope. Alert on changes to federated identity configurations for third-party relationships. Implement regular review of third-party access permissions and monitor for privilege creep. Track third-party account activity for behavioral deviations from established baselines.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: The SolarWinds supply chain attack demonstrated persistent access through trusted vendor relationships. Operation Cloud Hopper maintained persistent access through compromised managed service providers. The Kaseya VSA attack leveraged trusted MSP software for persistent access to downstream customers.

attack_patterns.name: Modify Authentication Process: Multi-Factor Authentication Interception (Persistence)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may modify MFA configurations at the identity provider level to create persistent bypass mechanisms for multi-factor authentication. By installing rogue MFA providers, modifying conditional access policies to exempt certain conditions from MFA, or adding trusted IP ranges that bypass MFA requirements, adversaries can ensure persistent access that is not challenged by MFA even after the user's primary credentials are reset. This technique targets the MFA infrastructure itself rather than individual user MFA tokens, providing broad and persistent bypass capability.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for changes to MFA provider configurations and conditional access policies at the identity provider level. Alert on modifications to Azure AD Conditional Access policies, especially changes to MFA enforcement conditions, trusted locations, and device compliance requirements. Track AD FS claims rules modifications that could bypass MFA evaluation. Monitor for new MFA provider registrations. Alert on bulk MFA method resets or re-registrations. Track changes to named locations and trusted IP ranges. Implement immutable logging for MFA configuration changes.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: APT29 modified AD FS MFA configurations using MagicWeb to create persistent MFA bypass during the SolarWinds campaign. Conditional access policy manipulation for MFA bypass has been observed in multiple cloud-focused attack campaigns.

attack_patterns.name: Impair Defenses: Disable Windows Event Logging (Defense Evasion)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may disable Windows event logging to prevent the collection of audit events that could be used to detect their authentication-related activities. Windows event logging captures authentication events (4624, 4625), account management events (4720, 4738), privilege use events (4672), and other security-relevant events. By disabling the EventLog service, modifying audit policies, clearing event logs, or patching the Event Tracing for Windows (ETW) infrastructure, adversaries can eliminate the audit trail of their credential access, privilege escalation, and lateral movement activities.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for Event Log service status changes and audit policy modifications. Alert on Windows Event ID 1102 (audit log cleared) and Event ID 4719 (system audit policy changed). Monitor for EventLog service stop events. Track modifications to audit policy GPO settings. Alert on ETW provider disabling or modification. Implement SIEM-based gap detection that alerts when expected event volumes from a system drop significantly. Monitor for processes interacting with wevtutil.exe or Clear-EventLog cmdlets. Forward logs to an immutable log store that cannot be modified by local administrators.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Ransomware operators routinely clear event logs and disable event logging before deploying ransomware. APT groups disable specific audit categories to hide credential access activities. The technique is a standard component of most post-exploitation frameworks.

attack_patterns.name: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Application Access Token (Persistence)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may use stolen or forged application access tokens, particularly long-lived OAuth refresh tokens, to maintain persistent access to cloud services and APIs. Unlike access tokens which typically expire in minutes to hours, refresh tokens can have lifetimes of days to months and can be used to continuously obtain new access tokens. By exfiltrating refresh tokens, adversaries can maintain ongoing access to cloud resources even after the user changes their password. In environments without continuous access evaluation, refresh tokens may continue to function until their absolute expiration, providing a reliable persistence mechanism for cloud access.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for OAuth refresh token usage from unusual IP addresses or client applications. Alert on token refresh operations for accounts that have had their passwords reset or sessions revoked. Track Azure AD sign-in logs for refresh token redemption events from unexpected locations. Monitor for access token acquisition patterns that suggest automated token refresh from adversary infrastructure. Alert on refresh token usage for disabled or suspended accounts. Implement continuous access evaluation (CAE) and token binding. Monitor for bulk token refresh operations across multiple accounts.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Stolen OAuth refresh tokens have been used for persistent access in multiple cloud security incidents. The CircleCI breach (2023) involved stolen OAuth tokens used for persistent access. Microsoft has documented threat actors maintaining access through refresh tokens after credential rotation.

attack_patterns.name: Account Manipulation: Additional Email Delegate Rules (Persistence)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may create email forwarding rules or delegate access to maintain persistent access to victim email even after the primary compromise is remediated. Email forwarding rules automatically send copies of incoming email to adversary-controlled addresses, providing ongoing access to sensitive communications without requiring continued access to the email account. Inbox rules can also be configured to move specific emails (e.g., security alerts, password reset notifications) to hidden folders to prevent the victim from noticing the compromise. These rules persist until explicitly discovered and removed, often surviving password resets and MFA changes.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor Exchange/Microsoft 365 audit logs for inbox rule creation and modification events. Alert on rules that forward to external email addresses or move emails to unusual folders. Track mailbox delegation changes (Add-MailboxPermission, Set-MailboxFolderPermission). Monitor for transport rule modifications at the organizational level. Implement regular automated scanning of all mailbox rules for suspicious patterns. Alert on rules containing keywords related to security notifications (password, reset, security, alert, MFA). Track mailbox delegate access grants to external or recently created accounts.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: BEC (Business Email Compromise) actors routinely create inbox forwarding rules for persistent email access. APT29 created email monitoring rules during the SolarWinds compromise. Inbox rule persistence is one of the most commonly overlooked persistence mechanisms during incident response.

attack_patterns.name: Phishing: Spearphishing Link (Initial Access via AiTM)
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries may send targeted spearphishing emails containing links to adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) proxy sites to steal authentication credentials and session cookies. AiTM phishing represents an evolution of traditional credential phishing where the phishing infrastructure acts as a transparent proxy between the victim and the legitimate identity provider. This allows the adversary to capture not only the username and password but also the MFA challenge response and the resulting session cookie, effectively bypassing multi-factor authentication. The victim sees and interacts with the real login page, making the phishing attempt harder to detect.
attack_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for phishing indicators: emails containing links to recently registered domains that mimic corporate login pages. Alert on authentication flows that pass through intermediate proxy servers (check for unexpected X-Forwarded-For headers, certificate mismatches, or unusual redirect chains). Monitor Azure AD for sign-in events with atRisk session risk. Track authentication events where the client IP differs between the MFA challenge and the session establishment. Alert on session cookie usage from IPs different from the authentication IP. Monitor for bulk phishing campaigns targeting the organization's identity provider login page.
attack_patterns.real_world_examples: Storm-1167 and DEV-1101 operate large-scale AiTM phishing campaigns targeting Microsoft 365 organizations. Evilginx2 and Modlishka are widely used open-source AiTM proxy tools. Microsoft reported AiTM phishing targeting over 10,000 organizations in a 2022 campaign.
